title: Trachomitum scabrum
taxonomy: Trachomitum scabrum belongs to the family Apocynaceae and is classified under the genus Trachomitum. The species is native to the tropical regions of Asia. Within the Apocynaceae family, Trachomitum scabrum is closely related to other species such as Trachomitum galactolepis. The classification of Trachomitum scabrum is further broken down into the kingdom Plantae and the phylum Magnoliophyta. It is a part of the larger taxonomic group called Asparagophyta.
uses: Trachomitum scabrum, also known as the 'Chinese cork tree,' has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. It is believed to possess antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, making it useful for treating fever, inflammation, and other related conditions. The plant's bark is traditionally used to treat various ailments, including rheumatism, arthritis, and skin conditions. Furthermore, Trachomitum scabrum has been used in traditional Chinese medicine to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and pneumonia. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal properties and potential uses.

These alkaloids have been found to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antimalarial activities. Trachomitin, in particular, has been shown to exhibit potent antimalarial activity against the Plasmodium parasite. intro: Trachomitum scabrum, also known as Huangbo, is a plant species within the Asclepiadaceae family, commonly found in tropical regions of Asia. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its numerous health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties, which can aid in reducing pain and inflammation, as well as boosting the immune system. Bioactive constituents of Trachomitum scabrum include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Trachomitum scabrum is commonly prepared as a decoction or a tincture, and is often used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues.
